name: Andromache
description: Andromache, a figure of immense sorrow and resilience in Trojan mythology, was the wife of Hector, Troy's greatest warrior, and the daughter of Eetion, king of Cilician Thebe. Her life was marked by tragedy, witnessing the deaths of her father and brothers at the hands of Achilles. As Hector's wife, she represented the ideal Trojan woman: devoted, loving, and concerned for her husband's safety and the future of her family. She famously pleaded with Hector not to return to battle, foreseeing his doom and the destruction of Troy, but his sense of honor compelled him to fight. Following Hector's death at the hands of Achilles, Andromache's son Astyanax was brutally thrown from the walls of Troy to prevent him from becoming a future threat. Enslaved after the fall of Troy, Andromache became the concubine of Neoptolemus (Pyrrhus), Achilles' son, and bore him several children. After Neoptolemus' death, she married Helenus, a Trojan prince and prophet, and they ruled over a new city in Epirus, where she finally found some semblance of peace. Andromache's story is a powerful exploration of the devastating consequences of war, the loss of loved ones, and the enduring strength of the human spirit in the face of unimaginable suffering. She embodies the virtues of loyalty, love, and resilience, making her a poignant and enduring figure in classical literature.
subtitle: Hector's Wife, Trojan Tragedy, Enduring Strength

keyMyths.title: Hector's Farewell
keyMyths.description: Andromache pleads with Hector not to return to battle, fearing his death and the fall of Troy. This scene highlights her love for him and her premonition of doom, as well as Hector's unwavering sense of duty.

keyMyths.title: Death of Astyanax
keyMyths.description: After the fall of Troy, Andromache's son, Astyanax, is thrown from the walls of the city by the Greeks, a brutal act intended to eliminate any future Trojan threat. This event underscores the utter devastation and cruelty of war.
keyMyths.source: Various accounts of the Trojan War

keyMyths.title: Andromache's Captivity
keyMyths.description: Following the sack of Troy, Andromache becomes a slave of Neoptolemus, Achilles' son, and bears him children. Her story reflects the tragic fate of many Trojan women after their city's destruction.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Primary source for Hector and Andromache's relationship and the Trojan War.
sources.text: Primary source for Hector and Andromache's relationship and the Trojan War.

sources.author: Euripides
sources.description: Details Andromache's life as a captive and her relationship with Neoptolemus and Hermione.
sources.text: Details Andromache's life as a captive and her relationship with Neoptolemus and Hermione.
